data.title: The Gap: A Discounted Turnaround Story With Great Fundamentals
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered The Gap (GAP), I highlighted their strong financials, attractive valuation, and resilient cash flow despite the ongoing macro headwinds, rating them a Buy.

With the stock falling following their plan for a significant jump in CAPEX this year and more macro pressure despite tariff improvements, the company’s long-term turnaround potential is even more impressive, backed by their strong cash flow and solid financial position that can support their improved shareholder returns alongside these investments.

Internal Developments

GAP reported an overall acceptable Q4 and 2025, with both the top- and bottom-line in the fourth quarter being in line with the market estimates, with a modest ~2% increase in net sales and a ~3.2% drop in EPS during the year, which is not terrible given the environment we’re talking about, as I’ll mention below.

Still, despite this ongoing macro weakness, we can continue to see a solid FCF, reaching $823 million in 2025 compared to $1.04 billion in 2024, although the hit came as a result of working capital impacts and a ~$23 million increase in CAPEX, and adjusting it for working capital changes gets us to about $985 million in 2025 and $1.015 billion in 2024, underlining an overall solid business even during the tariff/consumer pressure and winter storm effects.

As for 2026, GAP expects their net sales growth to continue in the 2% to 3% range, with most numbers remaining relatively flat or delivering slight growth, expecting their 2026 CAPEX to reach ~$650 million, a massive increase compared to the $470 million seen in 2025 and $447 million from 2024. Note that this guidance assumes tariff levels before the Supreme Court’s decision and the subsequent increase (about 200 bps on the gross margin in Q1).

This massive jump comes from their “fix the fundamentals” phase, aiming at developing new store formats and remodeling now that they're done with the closures (expecting flat net closures in 2026), on top of other AI, tech, and supply chain investments, aiming to develop their fashiontainment platform.

Financially, based on GAP’s latest report, we continue to see a strong position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and their long-term debt combined, while they hold a growing ~$2.62 billion worth of cash and cash equivalents plus $386 million in short-term investments. As they say, this is the highest cash balance in nearly 2 decades, providing significant levels of financial flexibility given their ~$9 billion market cap, especially since their debt is made up of $750 million worth of 3.625% notes due 2029 and $750 million of 3.875% notes due 2031.

As for the shareholder returns, GAP paid $155 million in buybacks on top of the $247 million dividend, with a 6% dividend increase announced now (to a yield of ~3%) and a new $1 billion buyback authorization announced. Given the free cash flow, the company should be able to pay significantly more if they want to, even if we take into account the higher CAPEX and macro pressure expected in 2026.

Macro Developments

To begin with the ongoing Iran conflict, this goes far beyond just oil/gas prices, and following Iran’s plans to close the Strait of Hormuz, the situation continues to degenerate, with Trump saying that he will not take deals with Iran except their “unconditional surrender,” pushing GAP down alongside the rest of the market, while Brent crude oil prices jumped above $90.

The President also said that the US Navy could escort ships, but that was far from enough to stop major shipping companies like Maersk (AMKBY) from not only avoiding it, but also the entire region (including the Suez Canal), taking the long Cape of Good Hope route instead, which can cause significant delays and costs in global trade.

This comes on top of an already pressured and uncertain environment, with elevated levels of fear, stress, and especially consumer weakness reported by virtually every company in recent years, as a result of a mix between the effects of rate hikes and the tariff situation.

Although we see some improvements both in terms of economic data and US consumer sentiment, this is still far from a great environment, with the latter, for example, plummeting to the weakest level since 2014 not long ago, and improving from a “slump” is not really that hard. Meanwhile, very recent data shows the US economy losing more jobs than expected in February, with the unemployment rate rising to 4.4% and cutting the market's turnaround hopes short.

Meanwhile, the Supreme Court’s decision regarding tariffs can be great for GAP (compared to the significant hit they saw from them in 2025), as even with the subsequent increase announced afterwards, we’re still talking about an improvement compared to last year, which the company’s CFO mentioned during their Q4 Earnings Call:

At this time, we expect any benefit to Q1 to be minimal based on the timing of receipts. In the meantime, our teams are continuing to leverage the extensive tariff mitigation strategies we've built out over the past year, which sets us up for the annualization of last year's tariffs to be net neutral to 2026 full year operating income as previously disclosed.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, given the industry we’re talking about, the consumer will always be the main pillar of the business, and the current environment certainly has room for it to deteriorate.

Although GAP’s financials support their resilience, their high CAPEX plans/turnaround initiatives also carry risks, while the current macro landscape is far from the best moment to take them, which I believe warrants a solid margin of safety.

Valuation

For GAP’s DCF to Equity valuation, given their 2025 FCF, the CAPEX hike, and the potential tariff tailwind following the Supreme Court's decision, let's assume an FCF of $850 million in 2026, followed by a gradual recovery to $925 million in 2027 and $1 billion in 2028 (assuming that their CAPEX gradually falls), similar to the level from the past couple of years. Given their strong balance sheet and cash flow, I’ll assume that the net borrowings will be zero, as they don’t need to issue/repay anything for their significant turnaround plans.

Thanks to their significant investments, international expansion potential, and macro improvements, let’s assume they can grow this at a 5% CAGR until year 10, followed by a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a more elevated 12% discount rate, to take into account a much better margin of safety, with 384 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $10.98 billion, for an intrinsic value of $28.59 per share, which is above the $23.45 at the moment of writing this even when using relatively conservative estimates, while the company’s “real” turnaround potential is significantly higher, especially if market confidence improves and the discount rate falls.

Conclusions

GAP remains a buy, backed by a more attractive valuation, strong cash flow, a very healthy balance sheet, and potential for great shareholder returns while near-term turnaround and macro pressure unwind.

There are certainly risks associated with this turnaround and the industry in general, but the company’s position and financials remain very solid, while the recovery can also be very solid.

data.title: Kaspi.kz: The Floor Is The Dividend, The Upside Is Turkey
data.description:
I've never found myself in a more complicated love-hate relationship than owning shares of Joint Stock Company Kaspi.kz (KSPI).

When I last wrote this about Kaspi, the company was at $107 a share, I described Hepsiburada as a potentially transformative acquisition for the regional juggernaut of the steppes. The statement still rings true, and I have been on the hate side of the equation. The stock is now trading around $73. That is a painful 32% decline, and I owe readers a candid accounting of what has happened, what I think it means, and whether I would add at these prices.

The short answer is yes. Let me explain why.

What Has Actually Changed Since $107

The most important distinction to make upfront: the business has not fallen 32%. The stock has.

Kaspi's revenue compounded from 889 billion tenge in 2021 to 4,046 trillion tenge in 2024, representing a four-year run of consistent 30%+ growth. I realize that the recent growth has come more from Hepsiburada and has yet to fall to the bottom line.

That is not a business in distress. The gross margin has held above 70% throughout, which reflects the fundamental economics of Kaspi's platform: once you have built the super-app ecosystem, the marginal cost of adding a payment, a loan, or a marketplace transaction is trivially small. Kaspi is still the best super app business in the world, in my opinion, and that business has only gotten better.

What has changed is the complexity of the narrative. The Hepsiburada acquisition brought Kaspi into Turkey, a market with a population ten times Kazakhstan's but also with 60%+ inflation not too long ago, currency volatility that would test any CFO's nerves, and geopolitical proximity to conflicts that make Western institutional investors nervous. The market has applied a discount, and that discount now looks excessive.

The Dividend Floor: Why I Think Downside Is Bounded

Here is the arithmetic that anchors my view. Kaspi has committed to distributing a substantial portion of its earnings to shareholders, and even on conservative forward estimates, the 2026 dividend yield at today's price sits at roughly 9%.

Think carefully about what that means. A 9% dividend yield from a business growing revenues, with 70% gross margins, in a market where competitive dynamics heavily favor the incumbent. The core business is growing closer to 10% organically, but I believe Kaspi will be able to reaccelerate growth this year. At some price, Kaspi simply becomes a dividend stock that happens to have growth attached to it, and I think we are approaching that price now.

The capital deployment into Hepsiburada and the working capital dynamics of integrating a Turkish e-commerce business that operates in a high-inflation environment have been a drag on free cash flow, which paused the dividend this past year. However, this is not a structural deterioration in the core Kazakh franchise.

Hepsiburada: Still the Central Question

When I called Hepsiburada transformative when KSPI's stock was at $107, I was making a bet on optionality. Turkey is a country of 87 million people with a growing middle class, improving digital infrastructure, and a domestic e-commerce market that was underpenetrated relative to peers. Kaspi's formula in Kazakhstan—build the payments rail first, layer the marketplace and financial services on top, create a flywheel where each service makes the others stickier—is a formula that should theoretically translate.

The question is whether it is translating.

The honest answer is, early signals are mixed, and the timeline has been longer than optimists (myself included) expected. Turkish macro has been the primary headwind. The lira's depreciation hits the USD-denominated value of Turkish earnings before they even reach Kaspi's consolidated financials. A business growing 40% in lira terms can still look like it is shrinking in the dollars that GDR holders care about.

I am not going to pretend I have special insight into Turkish monetary policy. I do not. What I will say is that the current inflationary episode in Turkey is not a new discovery. The market already knows about it, and yet at $73.11, it seems to be pricing in something worse than "Turkish macro stays complicated." It appears to be pricing in something closer to permanent impairment of the Hepsiburada thesis.

That seems wrong to me. The underlying Turkish consumer economy is not going away. And Kaspi's track record of building dominant super-app ecosystems from scratch in emerging markets is the closest thing to a proof of concept you can find.

Am I Catching A Falling Knife?

This is the question I asked myself before writing this update. The stock has had a vertiginous decline from its 2024 highs, and the natural instinct is to wonder whether the fundamental floor I believe exists is actually there.

Here is how I think about the downside. Kaspi's Kazakh operations are not at risk. That business generates the overwhelming majority of current earnings, has a near-monopoly position in digital payments and financial services in Kazakhstan, and faces no credible near-term competitive threat. The National Bank of Kazakhstan has been a relatively constructive regulator compared to what you might fear in a frontier market context.

The Hepsiburada bet either works over a 3-5 year horizon or it does not. If it does not, Kaspi is still a dominant Kazakh fintech growing in the mid-to-high teens organically, paying a 9% dividend at today's price. That is not a disaster. Still a reasonable investment.

If it does work, then the addressable market Kaspi can serve has expanded by an order of magnitude, and the current valuation will look like a patent absurdity in retrospect.

What Would Break This Thesis

Caveat emptor, because there are real risks here, and I want to name them honestly.

Kazakh political and regulatory risk - Kazakhstan's institutional infrastructure is more developed than it was a decade ago, but it is still a country where regulatory decisions can move quickly and with limited warning. A change in the competitive landscape for digital payments, forced pricing concessions, or a shift in the government's relationship with Kaspi's banking operations would be a meaningful negative. This has been a part of the recent story, with recent increases in corporate taxes being a headwind.

Turkish macro deterioration beyond what is already priced - If Turkey enters a genuine sovereign debt crisis or the lira experiences another round of severe depreciation, Hepsiburada's USD economics get worse before they get better. I believe the market is already pricing in meaningful Turkish risk at these levels, but I could be wrong about where the floor is.

Emerging market contagion - Kaspi trades as a GDR on the London Stock Exchange and as an ADR in the United States. When institutional investors sell emerging market exposure indiscriminately, as they did in 2022, Kaspi gets hit regardless of its fundamentals. The current geopolitical environment means this risk is not zero.

The Opportunity In Plain Terms

Kaspi is a business with 70%+ gross margins, strong revenue growth, a dominant competitive position in its home market, and a high-optionality bet on Turkey attached to it, trading at a price that implies a ~9% dividend yield in 2026. Emerging markets scare off a lot of investors. Further, anything near Russia, where shareholders all experienced a complete wipeout, invokes an extra degree of fear.

The stock is not cheap because anyone has found a flaw in the Kazakh super-app model. It is cheap because Turkey is frightening, because geopolitical risk premiums are elevated, and because of the slowing growth in net income. None of those factors are permanent. The dividend yield provides a meaningful carry while you wait for the narrative to improve.

I remain long. I believe the floor is the dividend, and I believe the upside is Turkey working. At $73.11, that trade-off looks more attractive than it did at $107.

data.title: Hard Assets In A Soft World
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA | Dovile Silenskyte | Nitesh Shah

February 20, 2026, was supposed to be a quiet Friday.

Then, the long-awaited Supreme Court tariff ruling hit.1 A decision that many had assumed would grind through procedural limbo instead arrived abruptly, reshaping at least perceptions of the near-term economic outlook in real time.

It’s important to consider that we are operating in a regime where policy risk is no longer episodic; rather, it feels more structural. Trade policy can reverse. Fiscal priorities can expand. Central banks can appear to be pressured. Elections loom. Geopolitics can intrude. And markets, which might prefer gradualism, are increasingly forced to digest discontinuity.

Why is this important? Because when the world feels unpredictable, investors reach for assets that feel anchored.

And that’s where gold, bitcoin and commodities could re-enter the conversation.

The Quiet Force Behind Gold’s Surge

In 2025, gold had its strongest calendar year performance since 1979.2 That’s not a typo.

For roughly a decade, gold behaved in ways that many macroeconomic models could reasonably explain. Such variables as the U.S. dollar, real bond yields,3 inflation expectations and investor positioning accounted for much of the year-to-year price movement.4

But 2025 looked different.

Two forces stand out as possible drivers bringing us to a different sort of regime.

First: geopolitics. When currencies are weaponized, sanctions expand and cross-border capital becomes political, reserve assets are reconsidered. The freezing of Russia’s sovereign reserves in 2022 was not just a headline; it was a signal. If fiat reserves can be restricted, then more neutral reserves gain appeal.5

Second: fiscal dominance.

Fiscal dominance is the idea that fiscal policy begins to dictate monetary policy. When debt levels rise high enough and deficits remain persistent, central banks face constraints. Their mandate may still include inflation and employment, with considerations of financial stability becoming unavoidable. If bond markets wobble or sovereign financing costs rise too quickly, monetary policy may have to bend.6

That bending, whether it shows through lower real rates, expanded balance sheets or preemptive easing, carries a quiet implication: currency debasement risk could rise.

While we believe that gold may not respond to headlines alone, its price could very well respond to a perception of erosion of monetary credibility.

Importantly, that kind of perception is not a concept confined to one country. Debt levels across many developed economies have climbed. Political appetite for fiscal restraint across many developed economies remains limited. Central bank independence, once assumed as a constant, is increasingly debated in public.7

If that is the regime shift underway, then gold’s big price move in 2025 may not be an anomaly. It may be repricing a new set of economic considerations.

Bitcoin: Scarcity by Design

If gold represents geological scarcity, bitcoin represents algorithmic scarcity.

The total supply is capped at 21 million coins. Issuance declines every four years through a process known as “halving.” Today, approximately 3.125 new bitcoin are issued every ten minutes. It is estimated that number will be cut in half again in 2028.8

Bitcoin supply is not influenced by politics, elections or central bank meetings. It is embedded in code.

And yet, bitcoin price performance has not mirrored gold’s recent strength. It remains materially below its all-time highs and has experienced sharp corrections over the past year.9

The reason, in our opinion, is less philosophical and more mechanical.

Crypto markets had accumulated leverage, in this context meaning people using borrowed money to potentially increase the magnitudes of returns on these investments. As volatility declined, and at one point we saw bitcoin’s price performance volatility falling below that of gold, traders increased their exposures. When liquidation cascades were triggered, billions were forced out of positions. Looking back over bitcoin’s price performance history, large liquidation events historically led to periods of consolidation before subsequent price recoveries.10 That pattern appears to be repeating, in our opinion.

Another narrative that dominated past cycles—the so-called ‘four-year halving rhythm’—also appears less reliable. In earlier cycles, large supply reductions materially tightened issuance. Presently, cutting issuance from 3.125 to roughly 1.56 per block is meaningful, but far less dramatic than earlier reductions from 50 to 25 or 25 to 12.5.11

In short: bitcoin’s future may be less about mechanical supply shocks and more about a shifting macroeconomic regime.

And here, the same themes that support gold resurface, such as currency distrust, fiscal strain, global reserve diversification, even if we don’t yet see major central banks actively buying bitcoin as they have been buying gold.

But, it’s clear that bitcoin behaves differently.

Substitutes or Complements?

Gold and bitcoin are often discussed in the same breath, the expression ‘digital gold’ is commonplace in 2026. Both are scarce. Both are alternatives to fiat currency. Both attract investors skeptical of monetary policy.

But it’s clear that their price returns do not behave in the same way.

Over the long term, their price return correlation has been minimal,12 Gold has tended to behave defensively. It has performed during recessions, financial shocks and geopolitical stress. It has competed, in many ways, with bonds, and competition with bonds may be an important future portfolio construction consideration.

Bitcoin, by contrast, has behaved more cyclically. It has historically thrived during liquidity expansion and growth optimism. It has drawn more speculative capital and has responded strongly to risk appetite.

If diversification is the only free lunch in investing,13 then assets that respond differently to macro shocks deserve consideration not as substitutes, but as complements.

Gold price movements may hedge monetary instability. Bitcoin’s price movements may offer asymmetric upside in a world rethinking monetary architecture.

Both, in their own way, are hard assets in a soft-currency environment.

Beyond Monetary Assets: The Case for Industrial Metals

While gold and bitcoin reflect monetary concerns, industrial metals reflect something else entirely: energy addition.

The world is not simply transitioning energy sources. It is adding energy layers.

Oil demand has not collapsed. Natural gas remains critical. But electrification, driven by electric vehicles, renewable infrastructure and data centers powering artificial intelligence, is expanding electricity demand at scale.14

Electricity requires metal.

Copper wiring for transmission lines. Aluminum for distribution infrastructure. Tin for soldering across millions of electronic connections. Grid expansion is metal-intensive. Data centers are copper-intensive. EVs require multiples of the copper found in internal combustion vehicles.

At the same time, supply constraints are emerging.

Major copper mines have experienced disruptions. New large-scale projects are limited. Aluminum smelting capacity in China faces structural caps. Several base metals are approaching supply deficits.15

The dynamic is straightforward: steady or rising demand layered onto constrained supply draws down inventories. That tends to be price supportive.

AI may dominate equity headlines. But its physical manifestation is copper, aluminum and tin.

Oil: Oversupplied, Yet Explosive

Energy remains the largest weighting in many broad commodity indices, such as the Bloomberg Commodity Index and S&P GSCI Index.16 Therefore, it may be important to take this into consideration for those looking at a potential broad commodities portfolio exposure.

Structurally, oil appears oversupplied. Demand growth is slowing. Electrification in large economies like China is displacing gasoline consumption. OPEC has gradually unwound supply restraint. Saudi Arabia appears focused on regaining market share.

Inventories, particularly in China, have appeared elevated.17

Fundamentally, that is a heavy setup that may not represent a catalyst for oil’s price to rise.

Yet geopolitics may intrude. Iran remains central to global oil flows. The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow chokepoint through which a significant portion of global oil supply travels. Any disruption would materially impact prices.

Current oil prices suggest only partial risk of a possible disruption due to a kinetic event being priced in.

Therefore, it is our opinion that oil is structurally pressured, price-wise, but geopolitically volatile, which could lead to a weak foundation for oil price strength with explosive potential upwards if geopolitical risks materialize.

We would note that, if investors are looking at broad commodities but seeking options with historically lower oil exposure than major benchmarks, the WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund (GCC) may be worth further study.

Hard Assets, Different Risks

We may be entering a period defined by fiscal strain, geopolitical fragmentation, energy expansion and policy volatility.

In that world:

  • Gold price performance may hedge monetary credibility risk.
  • Bitcoin price performance may hedge structural currency dilution and system distrust.
  • Industrial metals price performance may express the physical buildout of electrification.
  • Oil could remain a geopolitical wild card, and it’ll be important to watch the situation in Iran and the Middle East.

The real question may not be whether hard assets belong in portfolios, but rather which hard asset addresses which risk.

WisdomTree has a suite of strategies, termed ‘capital efficient’ that allow for exposure to gold with something else. Each of these does incorporate leverage, combining a 90% notional exposure to gold futures with a 90% exposure to another asset class. These strategies include:

  • WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Equity Strategy Fund (GDE): This strategy represents a 90% exposure to a portfolio of 500 large-cap U.S. equities, market capitalization-weighted, 90% notional exposure to a portfolio of gold futures, and a 10% exposure to short-term U.S. treasuries.
  • WisdomTree Efficient Gold Plus Gold Miners Strategy Fund (GDMN): This strategy represents a 90% exposure to a portfolio of gold miners, specifically the equities of these businesses, a 90% notional exposure to a portfolio of gold futures, and a 10% exposure to short-term Treasuries.
  • WisdomTree Efficient TIPS Plus Gold Fund (GDT): This strategy represents a 90% exposure to a portfolio of Treasury Inflation Protected Securities (TIPS), a 90% notional exposure to a portfolio of gold futures, and a 10% exposure to short-term Treasuries.

data.title: Rocket Lab: The Neutron Delay Is Real, But So Is The Space Defense Opportunity
data.description:
Rocket Lab (RKLB) stock has gained 1.2% since my last report, in which I upgraded the stock to buy, as I believe that the delay on the Neutron rocket program offers an opportunity for investors. In this report, I discuss the delay on the Neutron program, analyze the Q4 2025 earnings, and update my price targets.

Neutron Fuel Tank Failure Is Manageable

My view for the Neutron rocket has been that, depending on the nature of the fuel tank failure, the cost impact would be manageable with timelines shifting to Q4 2026 and potentially even Q1 2027. That view was largely confirmed during Rocket Lab's Q4 2025 earnings call.

The company detailed the tank failure, which was not driven by a design issue but rather by a manufacturing issue using a hand-laid manufacturing method. For new tanks, automatic fiber placement, or AFP, will be used, which eliminates this issue. Furthermore, AFP is cheaper than manually doing the layup work. So, that already shows that from a cost perspective, the cost impact is contained, and R&D costs are still expected to peak in Q1 2026.

The launch schedule did slip from mid-2026 to Q4 2026. Management intends to have a second launch rather quickly after the first test launch if there are no issues found during the test launch. However, I still believe that if the first launch happens in Q4, a derisked schedule would indicate a second launch in 2027 with a multi-year ramp-up trajectory for the Neutron rocket.

Having listened to the management call, I would say that the comments aligned reasonably well with my expectations.

Rocket Lab Sales Growth Drives Fixed Cost Absorption Leading To Higher Margins

Fourth quarter sales increased 16% sequentially to $179.7 million. Space System sales declined 9.1% to $103.8 million, driven by the timing of satellite platform milestones, solar power system timing, and supply chain dependencies. So, that will become a watch item going forward. Launch revenues increased 86% to $75.9 million as the number of launches increased from 4 to 7. This also indicates a more favorable revenue per launch for the quarter. We also see that improving fixed cost absorption is leading to higher margins, with adjusted gross margins improving from 41.9% to 44.3%.

For the full year, sales grew 38% to $601.8 million. Space Systems sales grew 30% to $402.8 million, driven by growth in satellite platform sales, component sales, and the SDA Transport Layer Tranche II program. Launch revenues surged 59% to $199 million, driven by higher launch activity and more hypersonic missions as part of the HASTE program.

Rocket Lab Increasingly Positioned As A Critical Vertical Integrated Defense Space Company

What holds for Rocket Lab is that it is increasingly positioned as a launch as a critical component in the space-based defense capabilities of the U.S. Key programs include the SDA Tranche III contract valued at $816 million, opportunities for the Golden Dome missile defense system, and hypersonics test capabilities through the HASTE program. Through various acquisitions, the company is now also positioned even better to provide defense solutions as a vertically integrated company.

The launch cadence for the Electron program is set to continue improving, with a 20% increase expected. Against 21 launches in 2025, that would indicate 25 launches. That is below the 28 launches we get from the annualized launch figure of seven launches in Q4 2025. The company targets launches every 11-13 days, which would indicate 28-33 launches per year. So, by 2027 the company should have that launch cadence set, and that should drive even higher fixed cost absorption.

The company’s backlog now is valued at $1.85 billion, with 26% being for launch services. It clearly shows that viewing Rocket Lab through the scope as solely a launch provider is not accurate, as I already highlighted in a prior report. We also note that 65% of the backlog is now for government customers, providing a backlog embedded proof that Rocket Lab is increasingly becoming a defense industry play.

For the first quarter, the company expects revenues between $185 million and $200 million with growth in the Space Systems and Launch segments with GAAP gross margins of 34%-36% on an unfavorable product mix and non-GAAP margins of 39%-41%. Adjusted EBITDA loss is expected to be $21 million to $27 million.

Revised Neutron Schedule Causes Manageable Slip In 2026-2027 Expectations

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

If we look at the financial metrics for Rocket Lab, we note that positive EBITDA has shifted from 2026 to 2027. I would attribute that to the Neutron delay. Free cash flow is still expected to be positive by 2027, but the free cash flow burn estimate for 2026 has increased. However, the expectation is that EBITDA and free cash flow will substantially improve this year and in the years ahead.

My view remains that Rocket Lab is a stock that you shouldn’t buy for near-term fundamental value. At current share counts, an EV/Sales assessment would show around 7% upside, and a price-to-sales assessment would show around 15% upside. However, the company has indicated in its guidance to model using 605 million shares outstanding, which would mean that Rocket Lab is currently fairly valued in the EV/Sales assessment with 8% upside against the price-to-sales assessment. So, I am maintaining my (speculative) buy rating for the prospects that Rocket Lab can offer for investors beyond 2030. What you currently see in the stock price is a high but acceptable entry point, not a near-term capitalization opportunity.

Conclusion: Rocket Lab Stock Price Is Better Aligned With Reality Now

Investors have previously adopted 2030 as the anchor point for Rocket Lab’s valuation. If we were to do the same, then we would see that the stock indeed seems to be fairly valued against an EV/Sales valuation with some upside using a more generic Price/sales approach. I believe that the current share price offers an entry point at a fair valuation for Rocket Lab. The risk to that fair value entry point is, of course, the Neutron schedule. If the schedule has major additional slips in the timeline, the fair value also decreases. However, I do believe that management comments on the fuel tank issue were encouraging in the sense that the manufacturing method was determined to be the cause of the issue, with a mitigation already in place. We also know that Rocket Lab is increasingly positioning itself as a defense contractor with significant growth opportunities for hypersonics and space-based solutions, and the company's backlog already reflects this.

data.title: Grocery Outlet's Turnaround Might Take Awhile
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Grocery Outlet (GO), I explained that it was a potential turnaround stock. Now, it sounds like its turnaround plan has run into some setbacks. Grocery Outlet decided to move away from its treasure-hunt model and become more like a regular discount grocery store. In my previous article, I said that this was a risk factor and the company risked damaging its brand if it did this. It now looks like Grocery Outlet’s customers might not have liked its recent changes.

Grocery Outlet did report double-digit revenue growth in the fourth quarter, but that was mostly because of the extra week in 2025. Its total revenue rose 10.7% to $1.22 billion for the quarter, but same-store sales were down 0.8%. Meanwhile, traffic was up 0.9%. So, it sounds like Grocery Outlet’s customers are trying to conserve their money right now, which isn’t surprising. Right now, other grocery stores are also saying that their customers are now making more trips to the store, but buying fewer products on each trip. But Grocery Outlet also reported an operating loss of $234.8 million for the quarter.

This setup creates a potential opportunity for value investors who can look past the company’s poor GAAP results. Grocery Outlet’s operating loss includes non-cash impairment losses. But the reason for these impairment losses is that Grocery Outlet is writing off the value of many franchised grocery stores that it plans to close. These store closures aren’t a good sign, so this stock could be a value trap. But Grocery Outlet could also make changes to its turnaround plan and recover. It sounds like its management team knows that the company made some mistakes recently.

Grocery Outlet Is Bringing Back More Treasure-Hunt Groceries

Bears think that Grocery Outlet might not be able to buy the inventory it needs in the future. Grocery Outlet needs to be able to buy groceries for very large discounts from food manufacturers to make its treasure hunt available. This opportunity might not last forever if food manufacturers and competing grocery stores get better at managing their inventory. But on the Q4 2025 call, Grocery Outlet CEO Jason Potter said, “Before I dive into what we're doing differently, let me just say, first off, that we're convinced that ample opportunistic supply exists.”

So, Grocery Outlet can still obtain treasure-hunt groceries. And the company may be able to turn around its falling same-store sales by stocking more treasure-hunt groceries and removing some of the other merchandise that it’s added recently. On the call, Potter also said, “First, we added DC capacity and improved the flow of goods by reducing inventories across nonproductive categories to ensure we have room for opportunistic product. Second, we've also made improvements to our internal forecasting to maximize opportunistic buying.” These adjustments could help Grocery Outlet post stronger same-store sales growth in 2026.

Grocery Outlet Can Still Grow at a Slower Pace

A few years ago, Grocery Outlet was considered a growth stock. This company is still adding stores at a relatively rapid pace. Grocery Outlet has around 560 stores right now and it’s planning to add 30 to 33 stores in 2026. But Grocery Outlet is now planning to close 36 underperforming stores in 2026 as well. In my previous article, I projected that this company’s store count would rise by 6.6% in 2026, but now it looks more likely that it will fall by around 1% this year.

Again, while the store closures aren’t a good sign, they may also be a one-time event. In the long term, Grocery Outlet is still planning to add more stores. But the Grocery Outlet CEO also said, “We are not fully exiting any state, and we believe we have a meaningful opportunity to grow in the East over the long term. However, it's clear now that we expanded too quickly, and these closures are a direct correction.” So, it sounds like Grocery Outlet’s growth rate will be slower in future years.

Grocery Outlet Is Now Offering Short-Term Deals on Its Own Brands

Right now, Grocery Outlet is in a tough position because it’s bringing back its old business model. It now needs to buy more brand-name discounted groceries than it was planning to purchase. It will take some time to find these groceries and bring them to its stores. So, in the short term, it’s going to offer deep discounts on the private-label groceries that are currently in its stores instead. These promotions will cost the store another $20 million. As the CEO said on the call, “It's a quantum of about $20 million, just to clarify. And what we're doing is we're using fresh products, in some cases, direct-to-store branded quality products, as a bridge.”

Short-Sellers Might Be Wrong About Grocery Outlet’s Supply Chain

Seeking Alpha now says that there’s a 25% short interest for Grocery Outlet. It sounds to me like short sellers think this company’s buyers can’t find enough low-cost groceries to stock its stores anymore, so its business model change was forced. But that might not be true. So, if Grocery Outlet does bring back more treasure-hunt groceries soon, which it sounds like it’s planning to do, it might post better-than-expected results. This is an upside risk for short sellers.

On the other hand, the main risk for longs is that the short sellers could be correct. If Grocery Outlet’s buyers can’t find enough deals to stock the company’s stores anymore, then the company will have to go forward with its new business model. And it sounds like Grocery Outlet’s customers might not be happy if that happens, and they might decide to shop at other discount stores instead.

Updated Comparative Valuation for Grocery Outlet

This time, I’ll compare Grocery Outlet to the major grocery store chains Albertson’s (ACI) and Kroger (KR) using non-GAAP P/E ratios. Using this metric, Grocery Outlet looks fairly valued in comparison to the other grocery stores. On March 6, a day after it released its report, Grocery Outlet had a non-GAAP forward P/E of 11.76. Meanwhile, Kroger had a non-GAAP forward P/E of 13.93, and Albertsons had a non-GAAP forward P/E of 8.06. While Grocery Outlet isn’t likely to grow in 2026, it may still have long-term growth potential that supports a premium multiple. Grocery Outlet has a smaller store base than the major grocery store chains, so it has more room to expand. And it’s bringing back the unique business model that gives it a moat, which is why it has higher margins than traditional grocery stores.

Updated Grocery Outlet Valuation

Grocery Outlet reported full-year revenue of $4.69 billion in 2025. Now, it’s guiding for revenue of $4.6 billion to $4.72 billion in 2026. So, it expects to report GAAP revenue growth of -2% to 0.6% in 2026. But this result might not be as bad as it looks. The company also benefited from an extra calendar week in 2025 that added revenue of $82 million, and it will have a 52-week calendar year in 2026.

This company also guided for same-store sales growth of -2% to 0% in 2026, which is -1% at the midpoint. And while it plans to open 30-33 new stores in 2026, it will also close 36 underperforming stores, so I’d estimate that its total store count will fall by around 1%. These factors add up to -2%. So, it sounds more likely that Grocery Outlet will report revenue growth near the bottom of its guidance range in 2026. If the company’s revenue falls by 2% in 2026, it would report revenue of $4.6 billion for the year.

Grocery Outlet’s gross margin was 30.3% in 2025. It also reported an adjusted profit of $75.2 million for the year, so its adjusted profit margin was 1.6%. Its 2026 guidance calls for a gross margin in the range of 29.7% to 30%, which is a drop of 30 to 60 basis points. If Grocery Outlet’s revenue drops in 2026, which appears likely, its margins will get worse because of deleverage on fixed costs. So, I’ll also project that Grocery Outlet’s net margin will decrease 50 basis points to 1.1% in 2026.

If Grocery Outlet reports an adjusted profit margin of 1.1% in 2026 with revenue of $4.6 billion, its net income for the year would be $51 million. The company had 98.2 million shares outstanding at the end of 2025, and it has other priorities besides stock buybacks right now. So, if its share count remains the same in 2026, its adjusted full-year EPS would be $0.51, which is near the middle of its guidance range of $0.45 to $0.55.

According to SA, Grocery Outlet currently has a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 11.76. If I multiply its $51 million in projected income by 11.76, I get a 2026 market cap of $600 million. Then, if I divide this market cap by 98.2 million shares, I get a one-year price target of $6.11 per share. Grocery Outlet’s current stock price is $6.59 per share, so my updated price target is 7% lower than the current price.

Conclusion

Grocery Outlet’s turnaround may take a while. In the long term, it might be able to generate revenue growth again by adding more stores and bringing back more treasure-hunt groceries. But the company’s guidance implies that it will report weaker results in 2026. Grocery Outlet is planning to close more stores than it’s opening in 2026, and it also expects negative same-store sales growth. It also expects its margins to get worse. So, I’ll maintain my hold rating for Grocery Outlet.
